briefs.title: Council to Consider: Territorial Acknowledgement at Burnaby Meeting
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to open its April 28, 2026 meeting with a Territorial Acknowledgement — a formal recognition of the Indigenous peoples on whose traditional lands the city sits. This is a standing ceremonial item on the agenda and does not propose any new rules or requirements for businesses.

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Burnaby Open Council Meeting Agenda — April 28, 2026
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to meet on April 28, 2026, to consider items on its open council agenda. The specific proposals up for discussion have not been detailed in the source material, so business owners should review the full agenda directly to identify any items that may affect their operations.
briefs.action_required: Check the full Burnaby Council agenda for April 28, 2026 to spot any items affecting your business.

key_dates.label: Burnaby City Council open meeting — agenda items considered

briefs.title: Council to Consider: North Road BIA Renewal Abandoned After Petition Threshold Met
briefs.summary: At the April 14, 2026 Burnaby Council meeting, several items were considered — most notably, the proposed renewal of the North Road Business Improvement Area (BIA) bylaw was abandoned after enough business owners signed petitions against it. Council also referred a Canadian Federation of Independent Business letter opposing a Provincial Sales Tax expansion on professional services to staff for further review.
briefs.action_required: If you operate on North Road, watch for updates on BIA renewal — the current renewal attempt has been stopped.

key_dates.label: BC PST expansion on professional services — staff review report back to Council
National Day of Mourning workplace safety event — Westminster Pier Park

The details of the specific proposals within these reports are not yet available, so business owners should monitor the full agenda for items that may affect their operations.

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby to Replace 60-Year-Old Zoning Bylaw with New Rules Effective July 1, 2026
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on scrapping Burnaby's current Zoning Bylaw 1965 and replacing it with a brand-new Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 2026. If approved, the new bylaw would take effect July 1, 2026, and would change how buildings are regulated city-wide — shifting from density-based rules (floor area ratios) to height-based rules — while also introducing new design guidelines, streamside protection rules, and tenant protection requirements.
briefs.action_required: Check if your property's zoning district is changing on July 1, 2026 — it may affect what you can build or operate.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 2026 takes effect — new zoning rules apply city-wide
Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 2026 — Preliminary Plan Approval transition period ends

briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Tenant

briefs.title: Council to vote on $2M transfer to Burnaby Housing Authority for Year 2 operations
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on releasing $2.0 million in Year 2 operating funding to the Burnaby Housing Authority Corporation (BHA) — a city-owned body created to build and manage affordable and market rental housing in Burnaby. If approved, the money would come from the City's Operating Housing Reserve and go toward running the BHA through its second year of operation.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the April 28, 2026 council decision if you work in Burnaby housing development or property management.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Council vote — BHA Year 2 funding approval

briefs.industries: Landlord & Property Mgmt, Construction & Development, Finance & Insurance

briefs.title: Council to vote on Burnaby's 2026 property tax rates, including a 2.9% municipal increase
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on the 2026 property tax rates for all property classes, including a 2.9% general municipal tax increase and a 1.9% increase for growth infrastructure investment (money set aside to fund new city infrastructure). If approved, a formal bylaw — a local law — would be passed to make these rates official before the provincial deadline of May 15, 2026.
briefs.action_required: Check your property class and review the proposed rates — your 2026 tax bill will reflect these if approved.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Rates Bylaw 2026 — Council vote at public meeting
Burnaby 2026 property tax rates bylaw — provincial deadline to pass
Hastings BIA — first tax remittance (30%) deadline

briefs.industries: Landlord & Property Mgmt, Retail, Construction & Development

briefs.title: Council to vote on appointing a new Approving Officer for Burnaby subdivisions
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on appointing Mark Norton, Manager of Development, as an Approving Officer — the city official authorized to approve subdivision applications (requests to divide or re-divide land). This would add Norton to a team of three existing Approving Officers, helping ensure applications are processed without delays when other officers are unavailable.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the April 28, 2026 council decision if you have a subdivision application pending in Burnaby.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Approving Officer appointment — council vote date

briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Professional Services

briefs.title: Council to vote on approving oversized retaining walls at 8012 11th Avenue, Burnaby
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on a Development Variance Permit (DVP) — a special approval that allows a property to bend a specific zoning rule — for 8012 11th Avenue. The permit would allow retaining walls on the east and west sides of the property to stand taller than the 1.2 m limit set by city rules: up to 2.43 m on the east side and up to 2.06 m on the west side. The walls were already built over-height and also cross onto neighbouring properties, so easement agreements with those neighbours would be required if the permit is approved.
briefs.action_required: Attend the April 28, 2026 Council meeting to have your say before the vote.

key_dates.label: Burnaby DVP #26-01 retaining wall variance — Council vote date

briefs.title: Council to vote on $4 million insurance contract renewals for Burnaby city assets
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on approving roughly $4 million in insurance premiums for 12 city insurance contracts, all brokered through Aon Reed Stenhouse Inc. and renewing May 1, 2026. The good news: the overall cost is about $180,000 (4%) lower than last year, covering everything from city buildings and equipment to cybersecurity, fine arts, and liability protection.
briefs.action_required: Watch the April 28, 2026 council meeting outcome — this affects city operating budgets but no direct action is needed from most business owners.

key_dates.label: Burnaby city insurance programs renewal date — all 12 contracts take effect

briefs.title: Council to vote on $4,000 for Heritage Commission members to attend Squamish conference
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on spending up to $4,000 to send two members of the Community Heritage Commission to the 2026 Heritage BC Conference in Squamish, BC (May 4–6, 2026). The funds would cover registration, hotel, meals, mileage, and parking, and are already budgeted under the city's Advisory Body, Boards and Commission budget.

key_dates.label: 2026 Heritage BC Conference takes place in Squamish, BC

briefs.title: Council to vote on $1M+ networking software contract for Burnaby's IT systems
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on awarding a contract worth approximately $1,047,545 (including taxes) to Insight Canada Inc. for Cisco DNA software licenses — the digital tools that keep the City's internet, Wi-Fi, payment systems, and communications running. The proposed three-year contract includes options for up to two additional one-year renewals.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the April 28, 2026 council decision if you supply IT products or services to the City.

key_dates.label: Burnaby networking software contract award — council vote

briefs.title: Council to vote on $1.96M furniture contract for five new Burnaby community facilities
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on awarding a contract worth roughly $1.96 million (plus a $196,260 contingency buffer) to Heritage Office Furnishings Ltd. to supply, deliver, and install furniture across five new city facilities opening over the next five years. The five locations include the James Cowan Theatre, Community Safety Building, Burnaby Lake Recreation Centre, Cameron Community Centre and Library, and Brentwood Community Centre.
briefs.action_required: Watch the April 28, 2026 council meeting if your business supplies furniture or fit-out services.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Council vote — New Civic Facility furniture contract award

briefs.title: Council to vote on $47,000 contract increase for Burnaby's external audit services
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on approving a $47,000 increase to the existing contract with KPMG LLP for external audit services. The extra cost covers additional audit work required by new public sector accounting rules — standards that govern how governments report their finances — that were not part of the original 2021 contract.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the April 28, 2026 council decision if you provide audit or accounting services to the City.

key_dates.label: Burnaby external audit services contract expiry — KPMG LLP

briefs.title: Council to vote on $384,893 contract increase for Burnaby Lake Recreation Complex ground studies
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on expanding a contract with WSP Canada Inc. to cover extra geotechnical (soil and ground condition) consulting work at the Burnaby Lake Recreation Complex. Unexpected poor ground conditions were found during construction, so Council will consider approving $384,893 plus a $73,500 contingency buffer to fund the additional engineering studies needed to keep the project on track.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the April 28, 2026 council decision if you work in construction or supply services to this project.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Council vote — Burnaby Lake Recreation Complex geotechnical contract change

briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to review and vote on a series of committee reports at its upcoming April 28, 2026 meeting. The specific recommendations within those reports have not been detailed in the agenda item, so business owners should monitor the full meeting agenda and minutes for any decisions that could affect local regulations, permits, or business operations.

key_dates.label: Burnaby City Council committee reports meeting date

briefs.title: Council to vote on $215,000+ in Spring 2026 community grants for Burnaby nonprofits
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on approving 31 community grants totalling over $215,000 for the Spring 2026 intake. The grants would support local sports teams, food banks, newcomer services, cultural events, and homelessness programs — with funds split across five categories: Recreation Sports, Operating, Initiative, Subsidized Use of City Facilities, and Food Security.
briefs.action_required: If your business sponsors or partners with any of these nonprofits, watch for the April 28 council decision.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Spring 2026 Community Grant — Council vote date

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby to raise recreation fees and launch Hotel Burnaby pricing
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on mid-year changes to Burnaby's fee bylaw — a bylaw is a local law that sets official prices for city services. A proposed bylaw would update Parks, Recreation and Culture fees starting June 1 and September 1, 2026, add room rates for the new Hotel Burnaby, and lower the age for a free Seniors Lifetime Card from 90 to 80.
briefs.action_required: Check the updated Burnaby fee schedule — new recreation and hospitality rates could affect your business costs.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Consolidated Fees and Charges Bylaw — Schedule F1 (Hospitality/Hotel Burnaby) takes effect
Burnaby Consolidated Fees and Charges Bylaw — Schedule F3 (Recreation and Culture fees) takes effect

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Tenant Protection Bylaw 2026 — Council to vote on new renter rules
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a proposed Tenant Protection Bylaw (TPB) that would replace the current Tenant Assistance Policy and extend renter protections to redevelopments that don't require rezoning. If approved, the bylaw — expected to take effect July 1, 2026 — would give the City new enforcement powers, including fines up to $500 and stop-work orders, to ensure developers provide displaced tenants with proper notice, financial compensation, moving help, and the right to return to a replacement unit at their old rent.
briefs.action_required: If you own or develop rental property in Burnaby, review the proposed bylaw now — it could apply to your next project.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Tenant Protection Bylaw 2026 — proposed effective date
Burnaby Council vote on Tenant Protection Bylaw — April 28 meeting

briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Tenant

briefs.title: Council to vote on Southeast Bikeway Upgrade: new bike lanes, signals, and traffic changes
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a package of cycling improvements called the Southeast Bikeway Upgrade Project. If approved, the plan would add protected bike lanes, upgrade traffic signals, create multi-use pathways near schools, and change traffic flow on 12th Avenue — affecting roads connecting Edmonds Town Centre, Big Bend, and neighbouring municipalities.
briefs.action_required: If your business is near the Southeast or Big Bend corridors, review the proposed road changes before the April 28 vote.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Southeast Bikeway Upgrade — Council approval vote

briefs.title: Council to vote on safer intersection design for Burnaby Town Centres
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on updating the Town Centre Design Standards to include 'protected intersection geometry' — a road design that adds physical barriers, set-back crossings, and refuge islands to keep cyclists and pedestrians safer where roads meet. If endorsed, the new design rules would apply to newly built intersections in Brentwood, Metrotown, Lougheed, and Edmonds as those areas are redeveloped.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the April 28, 2026 council decision if your business is near a Burnaby Town Centre redevelopment area.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Town Centre Design Standards amendment — Council vote

briefs.title: Council to Consider Burnaby Consent Agenda Items — April 28, 2026
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a package of items grouped under the Consent Agenda at its April 28, 2026 meeting. A Consent Agenda bundles routine or non-controversial items into a single vote — any item can be pulled out for separate discussion if a councillor requests it. The specific proposals within this agenda have not been detailed in the source material provided.
briefs.action_required: Check the full April 28, 2026 Burnaby Council agenda for items that may affect your business.

key_dates.label: Burnaby City Council Consent Agenda vote — April 28, 2026 meeting

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Brentwood Park Cannot Be Exempted from Provincial Housing Rules
briefs.summary: Council is set to receive a staff report explaining why Burnaby cannot exempt Brentwood Park from provincial small-scale multi-unit housing (SSMUH) rules — laws that require cities to allow triplexes, fourplexes, and small apartment buildings on most residential lots. Staff say provincial law does not allow this exemption, and while Council could amend the city's Building Bylaw to enforce private land restrictions (called Restrictive Covenants) city-wide, staff do not recommend it because it would slow down and add cost to building permits across all of Burnaby.
briefs.action_required: If you own property in Brentwood Park or anywhere in Burnaby with a Restrictive Covenant, watch for the April 28 council decision.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Council meeting — Brentwood Park SSMUH report received for information

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Burnaby Building Permit Activity Report for March 2026
briefs.summary: At its April 28, 2026 meeting, Burnaby City Council is set to receive a routine information report on building permit activity for March 1–31, 2026. No new rules or approvals are being voted on — Council is simply being asked to acknowledge the report, which tracks construction activity across the city during that period.

briefs.title: Council to Consider Heritage Documentation for Forest Lawn Memorial Park Chapel Before Demolition
briefs.summary: Council is set to receive — for information only — two heritage documents about Forest Lawn Memorial Park at 3789 Royal Oak Avenue in Burnaby: an as-found report (a detailed record of the Chapel building before it is torn down) and a Statement of Significance (a document explaining what makes the site historically important). These documents were required as conditions of a rezoning application that would allow the Chapel to be demolished as part of a redevelopment of the funeral home facilities.
briefs.action_required: If you have a connection to Forest Lawn Memorial Park, review the heritage documents before the April 28, 2026 Council meeting.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Council meeting — Forest Lawn heritage documents received for information

briefs.title: Council to Receive Burnaby's 2025 Solid Waste and Recycling Annual Report
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to receive the 2025 Solid Waste and Recycling Annual Report for information at its April 28, 2026 meeting. The report shows the city collected 74,489 tonnes of waste and recyclables in 2025, hit a 61% diversion rate (meaning 61% of waste was kept out of the landfill), and outlines upcoming programs like curbside flexible plastics pilots, expanded cleanup events, and possible higher recycling requirements for construction sites.
briefs.action_required: If you run a construction business in Burnaby, watch for possible increases to the minimum C&D waste diversion requirements.

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Burnaby's Update on Plant-Based Food Options at City Facilities
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to receive an update on food sustainability efforts at city-run cafes and restaurants. The report — up for consideration at the April 28, 2026 meeting — outlines steps already taken to add plant-based, vegetarian, vegan, and dairy-free menu items at locations like Riverway and Mintara, while keeping food services financially responsible and customer-focused.
briefs.action_required: If you supply food or services to Burnaby city facilities, monitor how plant-based menu demand may shift your contracts.
briefs.industries: Food Service, Grocery & Food Retail, Agriculture

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby to add pedestrian scramble signal near Metrotown SkyTrain
briefs.summary: Council is set to receive a report on planned safety upgrades at the busy intersection of Central Boulevard and the Metropolis at Metrotown underground parkade entrance. The proposed changes include a 'pedestrian scramble' — a signal phase where all traffic stops so people can cross in any direction, including diagonally — plus improved curb ramps, updated accessible audio signals, and removal of a right-turn island to reduce pedestrian exposure to traffic.
briefs.action_required: If your business is near Metrotown, watch for construction notices starting Spring 2026 — expect temporary disruptions.

key_dates.label: Central Boulevard intersection safety construction completion target — Burnaby

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Burnaby Bylaw Up for First and Second Reading — April 28, 2026
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to consider a bylaw at its April 28, 2026 meeting, where it will receive its first and second reading. In municipal law, 'readings' are the formal steps a bylaw must pass through before it becomes official — first and second reading means the proposal is still early in the approval process and has not yet been adopted.
briefs.action_required: Watch the Burnaby Council meeting on April 28, 2026 for details on this bylaw.

key_dates.label: Burnaby bylaw first and second reading — Council meeting

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Council to vote on adding hotel, restaurant uses and 362 units at Lougheed towers
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would amend an already-approved rezoning for two high-rise towers at 3900 Grand Promenade and 6950 Austin Road. The changes would swap some planned office space for hotel and restaurant uses, add 362 more residential units, make both towers taller, and shift affordable housing requirements to a nearby site called Carrigan.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council vote — this could affect your plans if you operate near the Lougheed development site.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Zoning Amendment Bylaw No. 7, 2026 — council consideration date

briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Hotel & Accommodation, Food Service

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Council to vote on 12-storey mixed-use tower at 6535 Kingsway
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a rezoning bylaw that would allow a 12-storey building at 6535 Kingsway in Burnaby. The proposed development would include 290 residential units and ground-floor commercial space facing Kingsway, built by Intracorp with a double-height retail storey at street level.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council vote — this rezoning could affect nearby commercial tenants and property owners on Kingsway.
briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Retail

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Rezoning at 2757 Norland Avenue to Allow Five-Storey Self-Storage Building
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed zoning bylaw (Bylaw No. 14810) that would rezone the property at 2757 Norland Avenue in Burnaby. If approved, the rezoning would allow a developer to build a five-storey enclosed self-storage facility on the site, designed by Architecture Panel, Inc. under the Public Storage brand.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council vote — this rezoning could affect nearby property use and local storage options.
briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Transportation & Logistics

briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a bylaw at its April 28, 2026 meeting, with all three readings scheduled in a single session. briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby bylaw would set new rules for e-bikes and electric kick scooters
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would update Burnaby's Street and Traffic Bylaw to formally include e-bikes (motor-assisted cycles) and electric kick scooters. The bylaw would set rules for where these devices can ride, where they must be parked, and would create designated parking zones managed by approved shared micromobility operators — companies that rent out e-bikes or e-scooters to the public for a fee.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council vote — if you operate near shared e-bike or e-scooter parking zones, this could affect your storefront access.

key_dates.label: Burnaby e-bike and electric kick scooter bylaw — council vote scheduled

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby bylaw would regulate e-bikes and electric scooters in city parks
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would update Burnaby's parks rules to officially include e-bikes (motor assisted cycles) and electric kick scooters. If passed, riding or parking these devices in any Burnaby park would only be allowed in areas specifically designated for that use.
briefs.action_required: Check which Burnaby parks will be designated for e-bike and e-scooter use before riding there.

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Bylaw to Add Fines for E-Bikes and Electric Scooters in Parks
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a bylaw amendment that would update fines for riding or parking e-bikes and electric kick scooters in areas of Burnaby parks not designated for that use. If approved, the fine would be $200 (or $160 if paid early) under the Burnaby Parks Regulation Bylaw 1979 — part of an ongoing e-bike and electric scooter pilot program.
briefs.action_required: If you use e-bikes or scooters for deliveries near Burnaby parks, watch for the council vote outcome.

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby 2026 Property Tax Rates Up for Council Approval
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on Bylaw No. 14814, which would set the property tax rates for all land and buildings in Burnaby for 2026. A proposed bylaw would also apply a special local tax (an extra levy charged to businesses in a defined area) to properties in the Hastings Business Improvement Area.
briefs.action_required: Check your property class and review the proposed mill rates — they determine your 2026 tax bill.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Rates Bylaw 2026 — council vote at meeting
